coredata.dc:title: Visible light photocatalytic degradation of dyes by Ag3PO4/g-C3N4/CQDs composite
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yijun
Ding, Meijuan
Li, Ziyu
Li, Mei
coredata.dc:description: To facilitate the realize high-value utilization of coal tar pitch, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using ball-milling-assisted H₂O₂ oxidation method with tar pitch as carbon source. Subsequently, Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs composites were prepared through the electrostatic self-attraction of CQDs, Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄. These composites were then employed for the photocatalytic degradation of dyes, including methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine B (RhB). A case study of MO, the degradation rate constant of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs reached 0.02469 min−1, which was 11 times and 1.6 times higher than that of g-C₃N₄ and Ag₃PO₄. It found that Ag₃PO₄:g-C₃N₄ mass ratio of 1:1 and 5 mg/L CQDs solution loading were optimal. Characterization analysis revealed the specific surface area of the catalyst was increased and optimized band gap structure after the combination of CQDs as the co-catalyst. The possible reaction mechanism was also expounded, Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs constructed Z-scheme heterostructure, with CQDs as electron bridge. In addition, the impact of catalyst mass, initial concentration, and pH on the degradation process was studied, as well as the cyclic stability of the catalyst. Based on capture and ESR tests, the main active substances involved in the reaction were in the following order: h+ > ·OH > ·O2 −. These experiments provided a new idea on the application of coal-based CQDs in the field of visible light catalytic degradation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) Ag₃PO₄, (b) g-C₃N₄, (c) Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5, (d-h) TEM and HRTEM images of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5, (i) and EDS mapping images of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5.
(a) XRD pattern of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5, Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄ and standard card of Ag₃PO₄, (b) FT-IR image of CQDs-GZ, CQDs-ZW, g-C₃N₄/CQDs, g-C₃N₄ and Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5.
(a-d) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and pore size distribution curve diagram of Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄, Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄ and Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5.
(a) XPS spectra for CQDs, g-C₃N₄, Ag₃PO₄ and Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5, (b-f) C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Ag 3d, P 2p and S 2p spectra for CQDs, g-C₃N₄, Ag₃PO₄ and Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5.

(a) UV–Vis DRS spectrum and (b) band gap diagram of Ag₃PO₄, Ag₃PO₄/CQDs, Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5, g-C₃N₄ and g-C₃N₄/CQDs, (c) PL spectra and (d) TRPL spectra for Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5, Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄ and CQDs.

(a) photocurrent response spectrum, (b) electrochemical impedance spectrum and (c) linear voltammetry scanning curve of Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄, Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5, (d) Zeta potentiogram of g-C₃N₄, CQDs, Ag₃PO₄.
(a-c) M-S curves of Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄ and Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5, (d) band gap structures of Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄.

(a) photocatalytic activity and (b) pseudo-first-order kinetic model of no-catalyst, CQDs, g-C₃N₄/CQDs, g-C₃N₄, Ag₃PO₄/CQDs, Ag₃PO₄ and Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs, (c) degradation of MO, RhB and MB, (d-f) optimum ratio and kinetics of photocatalyst.

(a) Effect of dye concentration on photocatalytic degradation, (b) effect of catalyst dosage on photocatalytic degradation, (c-e) effect of pH on degradation of MO, MB and RhB, (f) cyclic stability of catalyst, (g) XRD pattern of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 before and after the reaction.
(a) Effect of different free radicals on photocatalytic degradation of MO by Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5, ESR spectra of (b-d) h +, ·O2 −, ·OH.
Proposed photocatalytic mechanism for degradation of MO, MB and RhB in Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 system.
Specific surface area, pore volume and average pore diameter of catalyst.
TRPL attenuation spectral analysis of the catalyst.

head.ce:title: Visible light photocatalytic degradation of dyes by Ag3PO4/g-C3N4/CQDs composite

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To facilitate the realize high-value utilization of coal tar pitch, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using ball-milling-assisted H₂O₂ oxidation method with tar pitch as carbon source. Subsequently, Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs composites were prepared through the electrostatic self-attraction of CQDs, Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄. These composites were then employed for the photocatalytic degradation of dyes, including methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine B (RhB). A case study of MO, the degradation rate constant of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs reached 0.02469 min−1, which was 11 times and 1.6 times higher than that of g-C₃N₄ and Ag₃PO₄. It found that Ag₃PO₄:g-C₃N₄ mass ratio of 1:1 and 5 mg/L CQDs solution loading were optimal. Characterization analysis revealed the specific surface area of the catalyst was increased and optimized band gap structure after the combination of CQDs as the co-catalyst. The possible reaction mechanism was also expounded, Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs constructed Z-scheme heterostructure, with CQDs as electron bridge. In addition, the impact of catalyst mass, initial concentration, and pH on the degradation process was studied, as well as the cyclic stability of the catalyst. Based on capture and ESR tests, the main active substances involved in the reaction were in the following order: h+ > ·OH > ·O2 −. These experiments provided a new idea on the application of coal-based CQDs in the field of visible light catalytic degradation.

In this study, Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs was synthesized by the combination of coal tar pitch-based CQDs with Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄ via electrostatic self-attraction. The degradation rate constant of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 composite catalyst 0.02743 min−1, it was 12.2 times and 1.8 times higher than that of g-C₃N₄ and Ag₃PO₄, and had the highest dye-degrading activity and good cycle stability. Mott-Schottky was used to test the energy band structure of the catalyst. The positions of the conduction band and valence band of Ag₃PO₄ were 0.65 eV and 3.06 eV respectively, and those of g-C₃N₄ were -0.06 eV and 2.89 eV respectively. Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄ form a Z-scheme heterostructure which provided a possible electron transport path. PL analysis revealed that CQDs acts as an electron bridge to transport electrons in the catalyst. It was found by capture experiments and ESR tests that the holes played a major role in the degradation of the dye by Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs, and the electron played a synergistic role.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development and progress of industries, the problem of environmental pollution cannot be ignored, particularly the pollution of water resources, which poses a growing concern. Water pollution has received widespread attention due to its escalating severity. Industrial dye wastewater, characterized by poor biochemical performance and challenging degradation [1], poses a significant threat to both human health and natural environment, so the treatment of dye wastewater as a formidable challenge [2–4]. Photocatalysis is a kind of green catalytic reaction with mild reaction conditions and advanced oxidation reaction using solar energy [5–7]. It represents a green, clean and renewable energy with low cost and excellent stability. Thus, photocatalysis holds remarkable potential in degrading and treating organic pollutants in wastewater [8–10]. The development of numbers photocatalyst materials has been developed the repertoire of catalysts effective in both visible and ultraviolet regions [11–13]. Therefore, the study of a photocatalyst exhibiting strong photoresponse across ultraviolet and visible regions to degrade dye pollutants.

In recent years, silver-based materials have been widely used in the hot research of photocatalytic degradation due to their large surface area, diminutive particle size, superior physical and chemical characteristics, and excellent catalytic and optical properties [14]. Ag₃PO₄ is an efficient inorganic salt semiconductor catalyst with an indirect band gap of about 2.36 eV [15], and a direct band gap of about 2.43 eV [16]. Direct band-gap semiconductor electrons only energy absorption to directly transit to the conduction band, while in the case of indirect band-gap semiconductor, conduction band electrons require relaxation to reach the conduction band [17]. Ag₃PO₄ exhibits strong optical absorption and excellent photooxidation properties, facilitating the separation of photogenerated charges and the decomposition of pollutants under visible light [18,19]. Nevertheless, Ag₃PO₄ also has obvious defects, resulting in photologic corrosion under light exposure [20,21]. This issue can be weakened by adjustments to the microstructure, semiconductor coupling, or composite polymer, among other strategies [22]. In the current study, the catalytic properties of Ag₃PO₄ through its integration with other semiconductor catalysts. Within the realm of photocatalytic degradation of contaminants, significant strides have been made in the exploration of Ag₃PO₄ combined with other semiconductors such as H₃PW₁₂O₄₀ [23], TiO₂ [24] and g-C₃N₄ [25]. The g-C₃N₄ is a representative metal-free polymer semiconductor, distinguishes itself with a narrow band gap, typically around 2.7 eV. Comprised primarily of abundant elements C and N on the earth [26,27]. It offers a cost-effective and nonhazardous, making it an excellent material for environmental protection. The g-C₃N₄ has the advantages of stability and response to visible light [28,29]. Therefore, it finds wide-ranging use in the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants [30–32]. This study assumes great significance, as the construction of Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄ heterojunction holds the potential to ameliorate catalyst stability and enhance photocatalytic activity to a considerable extent [33,34].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) represent a novel zero-dimensional nano carbon material that has garnered considerable attention for its cost-effectiveness, low toxicity, and controllable preparation [35]. It can absorb both ultraviolet and visible light, effectively converting photons from low energy states to high energy states [36,37]. CQDs can modify semiconductor photocatalysts, which can exist as acceptors and mediators for photogenerated electrons [38], exhibiting excellent photogenerated carrier transfer ability [39,40]. It can enhance the light absorption performance of semiconductor photocatalyst [41], making them ideal candidates for the modification photocatalytic composites, consequently elevating their photocatalytic activity [42,43]. As integral components of the photocatalyst, CQDs can serve as active sites that combine with other materials [44], thereby improving the adsorption of pollutants on the surface of photocatalyst.

In this study, CQDs, derived from coal tar pitch as carbon source, are introduced into Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄ composite catalyst. This incorporation serves the dual purpose of catalyst modification to enhance photocatalytic performance while also enabling the high-value utilization of coal tar pitch. The photocatalytic efficiency against dyes such as methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB) and rhodamine B (RhB) are detected Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs composite catalyst, and the underlying mechanism is elucidated.

Silver nitrate (AgNO₃), ammonia liquor (NH₃·H₂O), sodium hydrogen phosphate (Na₂HPO₄·12H₂O), urea (CH₄N₂O), hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂, 30 %), methyl orange (C₁₄H₁₄N₃SO₃Na), methylene blue (C₁₆H₁₈ClN₃S), rhodamine B (C₂₈H₃₁ClN₂O₃), tert‑butyl alcohol (C₄H₁₀O), and ammonium oxalate (C₂H₈N₂O₄). All of the above reagents were analytically pure and are from Aladdin. The mild modified coal tar pitch used in the experiment came from Ningdong Energy and Chemical Industry Base.

Recirculating water type multipurpose vacuum pump (SHB-IIIA, Zhengzhou Greatwall Scientific Industrial & Trade Co., Ltd.), HJ-6A multi-head magnetic stirrer (LKTC-B1-T, Jintan District Xicheng Xinrui instrument factory), electronic analysis balance (FA1004B, Shanghai Yoke Instrument Co., Ltd.), electric blast drying oven (FX101–1, Shanghai Shuli Instrumentation Co., Ltd.), high power numerical control ultrasonic cleaner (KQ-400KDE, Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Ltd.), PH meter (PHS-3C, Shanghai Yoke Instrument Co., Ltd.) and multi-channel photocatalytic reactor (PCX50A Discover, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co.,LTD.).

As plotted in Fig. (1 a), the pre-crushed coal tar pitch was ground with a mortar, weighed 3.0 g coal tar powder, 60 g small steel balls, and 30 ml H₂O were carefully added to the ball mill tank. The ball mill operated at a speed of 500 r/min, with 10 min pause following 1 h of operation, and cycle 5 times. The resulting solid-liquid mixture was added to 20 mL of 30 % H₂O₂ solution. The mixture was stirred by magnetic force for 24 h at room temperature. After the end of the reaction, the coal bitumen based CQDs solution was obtained by filtration. The solution underwent evaporation and concentrated to about 10 ml, dialysis (1000, Da) for 24 h, freeze-drying at −40 °C for 36 h, ultimately yielding CQDs powder.

Weighed 10.0 g CH₄N₂O into a crucible with cover, moved to a Muffle furnace. Heating from room temperature to 550 °C at the heating rate of 10 °C/min, holding for 2 h. When cooled to room temperature, the crucible was removed and ground to pale yellow powder.

As illustrated in Fig. (1b), 2.2 g AgNO₃ was dissolved in 70 mL deionized water and A small quantity of 10 % NH₃·H₂O was added to create solution A. Weighed 3.0 g Na₂HPO₄·12H₂O dissolved in 70 mL water, which was solution B. Subsequently, solution B was carefully added drop by drop to solution A. The resulting mixture was stirred in dark field for 8 h, and then filtered to obtain solid. Following three rounds of washing with deionized water and ethanol, it was placed in an oven and dried at 60 °C overnight to obtain a brown silver phosphate powder.

As depicted in Fig. (1c), 0.2 g Ag₃PO₄, 0.2g g-C₃N₄ and CQDs solution were mixed, stirred for 6 h after 10 min of ultrasound. The resulting solid was obtained by vacuum filtration, put in the oven, dried overnight at 60 °C. This process yielded the catalyst powder denoted as Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs. To differentiate between catalysts prepared with varying CQDs solution concentrations, they were labeled as Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-x (x = 2, 5, 10, 15 and 20 mg/L).

The photoelectrochemical test of different catalysts was investigated on electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E). The electrolyte solution was 0.2 mol/L Na₂SO₄, the reference electrode was saturated calomel electrode (SEC), and the platinum electrode was used as the counter electrode. 5 mg of different catalysts were ultrasonic dispersed in the mixed solution (VNafion:Vethanol = 9:1) of 300 μL, and it was coated on conductive glass (ITO) as a working electrode as evenly as possible, with an effective area of 1 × 1 cm2. The light source for the transient photocurrent (I-T, 0.2 potential vs. SCE) was 300 W xenon lamp with 420 nm filter, linear sweep voltammetry curve (LSV, −0.6 V to 0.2 V vs. SCE) and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS, 0.2 potential vs. SCE) were tested at a scanning speed of 10 mV/s, the energy band structure of the samples was analyzed by Mott Schottky curve (M-S, −1 V to 0 V vs. SCE) at AC frequency of 1500 Hz.

In the experiment of photocatalytic degradation of pollutants, 0.02 g Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs composite catalyst was dispersed in 30 mL of 10 mg/L dyes (MO, MB and RhB) and transfer to 62 mL quartz reaction bottle, stirring in the dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption-resolution equilibrium. After sampling, it was put into a multi-channel photocatalytic reactor (PCX50A Discover, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Co., LTD.) for light reaction, in which the light source was 5 W LED lamp and the stirring speed was 250 r/min, and sampling was performed at certain interval. The photocatalytic particles were separated via filtration through nylon filter (0.45 μm), and the absorbance A (A may indicate the concentration of the solution) at 463 nm, 664 nm and 552 nm was measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (TU-1950, Beijing Purkinje GENERAL Instrument Co., Ltd.). The degradation rate was further analyzed and calculated. The catalyst participated in the degradation reaction was recovered and washed. This experiment was repeated five times under identical conditions to assess the cyclic stability of the catalyst.

The crystallinity of the various photocatalysts prepared was measured by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku INT-2000). The surface morphology of the samples was analyzed through scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS Sigma 300), while the microstructure was observed via transmission electron microscopy and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM and HRTEM, F200X). The surface functional groups of the catalyst were employed to characterize by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Electrokinetic potentials of different samples were measured using Zeta potential analyzer (Austria, Anton Paar Gmbn). N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms for each catalyst were generated with a surface analyzer (BET, ASAP2020M), and the pore size distribution and specific surface area of the catalyst were analyzed. The optical absorption properties of the materials were examined using Ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS, Shimadzu UV-2550). Excitation and time-resolved spectra of the photocatalyst were obtained using a steady-state transient photoluminescence spectroscopy spectrometer (PL, FluoroMAX-4). The active substances involved in the degradation reaction were examined through electron spin resonance spectroscopy (ESR, Bruker EMXPLUS).

The microstructure and morphology of different catalysts were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy (SEM, TEM and HRTEM). As plotted in Fig. (2 a), Ag₃PO₄ is a near-spherical nanoparticle with a rough surface and relatively uniform distribution, with a particle size distribution between 200 and 500 nm. Due to the small size of the nanoparticle, agglomerations occurred. As shown in Fig. (2b), g-C₃N₄ appears as a blocky structure with many holes in the surface. As seen in Fig. (2c), the surface of the bulk g-C₃N₄ has many Ag₃PO₄ nanoparticles aggregating. Therefore, it can be shown that the Ag₃PO₄ composite is on the g-C₃N₄ surface. Because the content of CQDs is less and the particle size is small, it cannot be observed in SEM images. Fig. (2d-h) shows the TEM image of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5. As shown in Fig. (2d and e), Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄ are tightly combined. In Fig. (2f), the catalyst is crystallized well and there are very small CQDs nanoparticles attached to the surface of the catalyst. Fig. (2g and h) shows the closely phase interface among the Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄ and CQDs, in the red circle is CQDs with obvious lattice stripes, lattice spacing is approximately 0.192 nm [45], and calculated fluorescence quantum yield of CQDS is 2.75 % [46]. The black part is mainly Ag₃PO₄, which has a lattice spacing about 0.244 nm, corresponding to the (211) diffraction plane of the XRD pattern [47]. Fig. (2i) gives the EDS mapping image, in which Ag, C, N, P and S elements are detected respectively, and S elements may be derived from CQDs, consistent with XPS results.

The crystal phase structure of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5, Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄ was determined by XRD, is presented in Fig. (3 a). The pure Ag₃PO₄ catalyst exhibited a characteristic peak generally consistent with previous reports [18], featuring its strongest diffraction peak at 2θ = 3.11°, which corresponds to the body-centered cubic phase of Ag₃PO₄ (JCPDS No.06–0505). Whereas g-C₃N₄ (JCPDS No. 87–1526) displayed two diffraction peaks centered at 27.45° and 12.99°, corresponding to the (002) and (100) crystal planes, respectively. The strong diffraction peak at 27.45° is associated to the interlayer deposition of the 0.326 nm conjugate aromatic system, while the weak diffraction peak at 12.99° is associated with the in-plane stacking of the 3-S-triazine unit at 0.675 nm [32,48]. In the XRD spectra of the composite catalyst showed characteristic peaks from both Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄. Combining the SEM and TEM images, it is concluded that Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄ are successfully combined.

The surface functional groups of the prepared CQDs, g-C₃N₄, g-C₃N₄/CQDs and Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 were characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. As exhibited in Fig. (3b), a broad peak in g-C₃N₄ at 3400∼2800 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, the strong absorption peak at 1820∼1020 cm−1 is probably due to the stretching vibrations of CN/C = N, the sharp peak at 813 cm−1 belongs to the vibrations of the triazine ring [48]. The wide characteristic absorption band at 3440∼3000 cm−1 is the OH tensile vibration of CQDs, and the absorption peaks at 1560∼1340, 1236, 900 cm−1 are respectively attributed to C=C, C=O, CH [49,50], and there are broad peaks of S-S stretching vibration near 550 cm−1 [51]. By comparing the infrared spectra of two types of CQDs, it was found that the CQDs prepared using Ningdong medium temperature asphalt as carbon source exhibited a more pronounced peak compared to those prepared by Ningdong modified asphalt. By loading CQDs onto the surface of g-C₃N₄ resulted in a stronger peak of g-C₃N₄/CQDs compared to g-C₃N₄, which indicated that the loading of CQDs increased the asymmetry of catalyst structure. The FT-IR spectra of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 has shown characteristic peaks at 3460∼2830 cm−1, 1637, 1575, 1461, 1410, 1319 and 1242 cm−1, which are OH/NN, =CH, C = N, CN, CO-H, C = O and CH. Peaks in 975∼1054 cm−1 are attributed to phosphate [25]. The results indicated that the peak of g-C₃N₄ shifted from its original position to a higher frequency in Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 composite catalyst, providing evidence of the static induction effect of g-C₃N₄ and Ag₃PO₄ in carbon matrix [52]. The FT-IR spectra of CQDs, g-C₃N₄, g-C₃N₄/CQDs and Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 displayed similar characteristic bands, further confirming the successful preparation of binary and ternary nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of specific surface area and pore size of catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The N2 adsorption-to-desorption isotherms of the different catalysts are given in Fig. (4 a-d). These isotherms provided information about the specific surface area and pore size distribution of four samples Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄, Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄ and Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5. After examination, the isotherm of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 composite catalyst is type IV(a) isotherm [53], with the presence of a hysteresis loop of type H3. The mesoporous structure of the catalyst was analyzed [54] that the predominant pore structure in the catalyst was mainly a slit-like configuration.

The adsorption properties of the different catalyst samples are shown in Table 1 . The specific surface areas of Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄, Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄ can be obtained by using BET equation, as follows: 4.456 m²/g, 55.842 m²/g, 33.080 m²/g. Due to the large diameter of Ag₃PO₄ nanoparticles, the combination of Ag₃PO₄ nanoparticles with g-C₃N₄ may cover the slit holes formed by calcination on the surface of g-C₃N₄ [29], so the phenomenon of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄ specific surface area reduction appears. Compared with Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄, the specific surface area of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 is increased to 48.453 m²/g. Therefore, the loading of CQDs may increase the specific surface area and the active site of the catalyst.

XPS was employed to characterize the elemental composition of CQDs, g-C₃N₄, Ag₃PO₄ and Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5, the full XPS spectrum is plotted in Fig. (5 a). The Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 composite catalyst exhibited the main composition elements consistent with Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄ and CQDs, including Ag, P, O, C, N and S, which aligned with TEM results. Specifically, in the C 1s spectra Fig. (5b), the curve for C 1s in g-C₃N₄ could be fitted to two peaks at 284.8 eV and 288.2 eV, corresponding to the CC/C=C bond and NC=N bond [25]. In CQDs, a new peak at 286.3 eV emerged, attributable to the presence of COH bond [28], which was also detected in composite catalysts. Therefore, the CC/C=C bond in the composite catalyst primarily originated from CQDs, while the NC=N bond primarily stemmed from g-C₃N₄. Fig. (5c) gives the N 1s spectra of the different catalysts, with both Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 and g-C₃N₄ showing two peaks around 398.7 eV and 400.6 eV, indicative of the C=NC and CN-H bonds [55]. In CQDs, the corresponding characteristic peaks were located at 399.1 eV and 401.0 eV [56], with a shift of 0.4 eV, demonstrating the reduction effect of CQDs. As exhibited in Fig. (5d) for the O 1s spectrum, the peak at 532.1 eV in CQDs was attributed to surface-absorbed water molecules [57], while two peaks at 532.2 eV and 530.7 eV in Ag₃PO₄ were attributed to surface hydroxyl [15] and O2− [58]. Similar peaks were detected in the composite catalyst, indicating that the O elements mainly originated from CQDs and Ag₃PO₄. As shown in Fig. (5e), the Ag 3d peak was split into two peaks: 368.1 eV and 374.1 eV, representing the photoelectronic energy levels of the Ag+ chemical state and corresponding to the 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 orbitals, respectively [22,52]. The Ag+ peak in the composite catalyst shifted by approximately 0.5 eV, suggesting that the composite g-C₃N₄ and CQDs facilitated REDOX action, electron transfer on the Ag₃PO₄. Fig. (5f) shows the P 2p spectrum, where the peak of P5+ in Ag₃PO₄ is located at 132.7 eV [59,60], and offset of about 0.5 eV in the composite catalyst as the Ag 3d spectrum. There was S element in coal tar pitch. According to EDS results, self-doping of S element occurred during the preparation of CQDs. The existence of S element can optimize the electronic structure of CQDs, so the existence was further analyzed in XPS. As plotted in Fig. (5g), by analyzing the S 2p spectrum, the peaks in CQDs closely resembled those in the composite catalyst. The peaks at 169.3 eV, 167.9 eV, 165.3 eV and 162.4 eV/161.8 eV were attributed to C-SO3 [61], S=O [62], C-S-C [63] and S 2p3/2 [64], respectively. This indicates that the S elements in CQDs were derived from coal tar-based CQDs.

UV–Vis DRS was employed to characterize the photoadsorption properties of the catalyst. As given in Fig. (6 a), Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 exhibits the intrinsic absorption band of g-C₃N₄ in the UV region (250–450 nm). Due to the conjugated structure of CQDs, they exhibit varying degrees of extended absorption tails in the visible region (450–800 nm) [65]. Moreover, Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 exhibits the highest absorbance in both regions, indicating that the composite catalyst could more effectively harness light energy in the process of pollutant degradation [59,66]. Band gap energy values for different catalysts were calculated based on the spectral results using the Kulblka-Munk function, as illustrated in Fig. (6b). Eq. (1) is as follows:

After the combination of CQDs, the band gap energy (Eg) values of g-C₃N₄/CQDs (2.92 eV) and Ag₃PO₄/CQDs (2.36 eV) are lower than those of the pristine g-C₃N₄ (2.95 eV) and Ag₃PO₄ (2.42 eV). A narrower band gap makes the catalyst more susceptible to photoexcited, suggesting that the incorporation of CQDs can effectively adjust the band structure of the catalyst and may improve the photocatalytic activity. Through the synergistic interaction of Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄ and CQDs, the band gap of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 (2.83 eV) is optimized, leading to improved catalytic efficiency.

To assess the charge separation rate, photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) test was performed on the catalyst, as depicted in Fig. (6c). In comparison to the individual catalyst, the Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 composite catalyst exhibited the lowest fluorescence intensity at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, substantiating a reduced photogenerated e − - h + recombination efficiency [65]. The strong peak in g-C₃N₄ arises from the recombination of photogenic e − - h + pairs within the π-conjugated structure [67]. After recombination with CQDs, the peak intensities of both g-C₃N₄ and Ag₃PO₄ decreased, and a blue shift in the peak position was observed. This phenomenon suggests that CQDs may serve as electron bridges, facilitating electron transfer and inhibiting the recombination of e − - h + pairs. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime of photogenerated carriers in the catalyst was measured using transient photoluminescence spectroscopy (TRPL) decay spectroscopy analysis. The attenuation kinetics were fitted as illustrated in Fig. (6d) as shown in Eq. (2), with the average life of the catalyst calculated using by Eq. (3):

The calculated results, as presented in Table 2 , reveal that Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 possesses a longer average lifetime τave (0.25 ns) compared to Ag₃PO₄ (0.06 ns) and CQDs (0.03 ns). Consequently, the charge transfer rate is higher [68,69], which potentially provides more opportunities for e − - h + pairs to participate in the degradation of organic ubstances.

Photoelectrochemical characterization of different catalysts was conducted. As presented in Fig. (7 a), I-T were carried out for Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄ and Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5. After four cycles of light and dark exposure, the order of photocurrent response intensity under visible light irradiation is as follows: Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 > Ag₃PO₄ > g-C₃N₄. When compared to Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄, Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 exhibited an increased current density and displayed the maximum photocurrent response. Additionally, the transient photocurrent of all photocatalysts proved to be reversible and reproducible, indicating that the catalysts possessed good photoelectrochemical stability. In Fig. (7b), EIS was employed to further investigate the charge transfer capabilities of different catalysts under visible light irradiation [70]. The order of curvature radius is as follows: g-C₃N₄ > Ag₃PO₄ > Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5. The arc radius of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 was the smallest, suggesting that it had the least charge transfer resistance. CQDs provided the electron transport pathway, leading to the highest charge transfer efficiency of the composite catalyst, which aligned with the transient photocurrent results. LSV was used to test the current density of different catalysts [71]. As exhibited in Fig. (7c), the photocurrent density of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 was significantly stronger than that of the individual Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄ catalysts. Consequently, Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 exhibited higher charge transfer efficiency. These results demonstrate that the photoelectrochemical properties of the Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 composite are superior, enabling more effective pollutant degradation.

The Zeta potential of the individual catalysts Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄, CQDs is shown in Fig. (7d). Ag₃PO₄ had a potential of −52.34 mV, g-C₃N₄ had a potential of 7.39 mV, and CQDs had a potential of −4.34 mV. Given that g-C₃N₄ carries a positive charge while Ag₃PO₄ and CQDs bear a negative charge, these three catalysts can be effectively prepared as new catalysts through electrostatic attraction.

The M-S method was employed to determine the flat-band potential and semiconductor type of the samples. In Fig. (8 a-c), the positive slope of the prepared photocatalysts indicates that they are all n-type semiconductor [72,73]. The intersection of the tangent line of the C−2 curve with the potential in the figure represents the flat-band edge potential (Efb) of the samples [74,75]. According to the figure, the Efb values for Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄ and Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 are 0.61, −0.10, and 0.14 V, respectively. Utilizing Eq. (4):

The conduction band (CB) edge potential (ECB) of the photocatalyst can be calculated. The ECB values of Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄ and Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 are 0.65, −0.06 and 0.18 eV, respectively. According to Eq. (5) for calculating the valence band edge potential:

Combined with the analysis results of UV–Vis diffuse reflection, the calculated EVB values for Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄ and Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 are 3.06, 2.89 and 3.01 eV, respectively, and the bandgap structure is exhibited in Fig. (8d). This Z-scheme heterojunction may be provided a possible charge transfer pathway [78].

In this study, the photocatalytic activities of different catalysts were investigated by conducting degradation experiments using catalyst-free, single catalyst, binary catalyst and ternary catalyst systems, respectively. The solid catalysts were all used at 0.02 g, the CQDs solution was added proportionally. The pollutants were in the form of 30 mL solutions with a concentration of 10 mg/L. As shown in Fig. (9 a), while the degradation efficiency of g-C₃N₄/CQDs is slightly improved compared to g-C₃N₄, the degradation rates for both catalysts only reached about 35 % after 120 min of photoreaction. The degradation effect of Ag₃PO₄/CQDs is improved compared to Ag₃PO₄, but the degradation rate of Ag₃PO₄/CQDs is only about 92 % after 120 min photoreaction. On the other hand, the degradation effect of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs is not only better than Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄, but also achieved a degradation rate of 96 % after 30 min of light exposure and 99 % after 120 min of light exposure. Pseudo-first-order kinetic model was employed to simulate the degradation kinetics of different catalysts, as expressed in Eq. (6):

[ 79 ]

The kinetics curve is exhibited in Fig. (9b). The rate constant of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs is 0.02469 min−1, which is 11 times that of pure g-C₃N₄ and 1.6 times that of pure Ag₃PO₄. The optimal degradation activity of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs can be attributed to the highly dispersed CQDs on the surface of the Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄ catalyst, providing efficient electron transport channels. The combination Ag₃PO₄ with the semiconductor g-C₃N₄, under light excitation, enhance charge separation efficiency, accelerates the surface reaction kinetics of the catalyst, mitigates the photocorrosion of Ag₃PO₄, and effectively improves the activity and stability of the catalyst.

In this study, equal volumes of different dyes were degraded using an equivalent amount of composite catalyst, as exhibited in Fig. (9c). The composite catalyst demonstrated the photocatalytic degradation of three dye solutions: MO, MB and RhB. The MB could be completely degraded in about 30 min from the start of photoreaction. Within 30 min, the degradation rate of MO solution reached approximately 95 %, and RhB achieved a similar effect after about 60 min of the photoreaction, although its degradation efficiency was slightly lower than the other two dye solutions.

To determine the optimal catalysts ratio for photocatalytic degradation, catalysts with Ag₃PO₄:g-C₃N₄ mass ratio of 3:1, 2:1, 1:1, 1:2 and 1:3 were prepared and combined with an equal volume of CQDs solution, which are divided into 10 mg/L MO solution for specific degradation of 30 mL, as shown in Fig. (9d). The best degradation effect for MO was achieved when the mass ratio of Ag₃PO₄:g-C₃N₄ was 1:1, with a degradation rate reaching d approximately 97 % after 120 min of light exposure. Based on these optimal experimental ratios for Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄, composite catalysts were prepared with 2, 5, 10, 15 and 20 mg/L CQDs solutions, and photocatalytic degradation experiments were conducted to determine the optimal proportion of CQDs in the composite catalyst.

The degradation rate curve is plotted in Fig. (9e), while the kinetics curve is exhibited in Fig. (9f). The composite catalyst obtained by adding 5 mg/L CQDs solution exhibited the best photocatalytic effect, with a degradation rate reaching 96 % within 30 min and 99 % after 120 min. The degradation rate constant was 0.02743 min−1, making it 12.2 times and 1.8 times higher than that of g-C₃N₄ and Ag₃PO₄. Excessive CQDs content may cover the active site on the catalyst surface, while insufficient CQDs may not provide enough active site, both of which are detrimental to dye degradation. Characterization analysis revealed that Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 possesses a large specific surface area, high charge transfer efficiency, and excellent photoelectric chemical properties, making it the most effective catalyst.

Based on the previous experiments, MO was used as a representative dye to investigate the influence of its concentration on photocatalytic degradation. Photocatalytic degradation experiment was conducted by adding the same quantity of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 composite material to solutions containing 10, 20, 40, 60 and 80 mg/L MO, respectively. As exhibited in Fig. (10 a), the most effective degradation occurred when the MO concentration was 10 mg/L, achieving a degradation rate of approximately 94 % after 30 min of exposure to light and about 99 % after 120 min. At MO concentration of 20, 40, 60, 80 mg/L, the degradation rates were 88 %, 71 %, 49 % and 32 % after 120 min of photoreaction, respectively. While the degradation rate decreased gradually with the increasing pollutant concentration, the composite catalyst continued to degrade MO at different concentrations under light conditions. Furthermore, the degradation rate increased with prolonged exposure to light, demonstrating the catalyst's ability for sustainable pollutant degradation.

Photocatalytic degradation experiments were conducted by introducing 0.01, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08 and 0.10 g Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 composites into 30 mL of 10 mg/L MO solution, respectively, to investigate the impact of the catalyst's quantity on photocatalytic degradation performance. It can be seen from Fig. (10b), nearly all degradation rates exceeded 95 % after 60 min of illumination and exceeded 98 % after 120 min. The degradation rate of MO increased with an increase in the amount of catalyst. At 0.01 g of catalyst, degradation was essentially completed after 1.5 h of photoreaction, albeit at a slightly slower rate. When the catalyst amount ranged from 0.06 to 0.10 g, the degradation rate was very rapid, to the extent that the catalyst could not participate fully, resulting in the wastage of catalyst. At catalyst dosages of 0.04 g and 0.02 g, the degradation effect was basically similar, with degradation rates reaching about 96% within 60 min of light exposure. Taking into consideration economic efficiency, 0.02 g of catalyst conserved raw materials, and thus, it was selected for the degradation experiment.

The pH of organic dyes, including MO, MB and RhB, was adjusted to 3, 5, 7, 9 and 11, and 0.02 g of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 was introduced into each solution for photocatalytic degradation. The influence of pH of solution on the photocatalytic degradation was investigated. It can be seen from Fig. (10c), when the pH = 3, the degradation of MO was optimal, and the degradation of MO diminished as the solution's pH increased, indicating that the alkaline environment inhibits MO degradation. As exhibited in Fig. (10d), the degradation of MB was most efficient at pH = 11, and the degradation of MB improved as the pH of the solution increased, suggesting that an acidic environment hinders MB degradation. As given in Fig. (10e), RhB exhibited good degradation in a neutral to acidic environment, while a strong alkali environment hindered RhB degradation. In strongly acidic and alkaline environments, the excessive H + and OH− ions reacted with electrons and holes, reducing the active substances involved in the degradation reaction, consequently limiting the photocatalytic activity to some extent.

To assess the catalyst's cyclic stability, degradation experiment was conducted under the optimal conditions mentioned earlier: 30 min of dark reaction followed by 40 min of light reaction. Samples were collected every 10 min once the light reaction commenced, and the catalyst was diligently recovered after each reaction. The test results are plotted in Fig. (10f). After 5 cycles of experiments, the degradation capacity of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 for dyes did not significantly decrease, and the degradation rate remained above 94 %, demonstrating the excellent cyclic stability of the Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 composite catalyst. To further corroborate the stability of the catalyst, the XRD patterns were obtained from the catalyst before and after the reaction. Fig. (10g) shows the XRD patterns of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5. It was found that the crystal structure of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 underwent minimal change. Consequently, it can be inferred that the stability of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5.

To investigate the roles of different active substances in the photocatalytic degradation process, free radical capture experiments were conducted during the degradation of dyes using Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 as shown in Fig. (11 a). The free radical scavengers of e −, h +, ·O2 − and ·OH were AgNO3, ammonium oxalate (AO), N2 and tert‑butanol (TBA) [79]. Upon the addition of AO, the degradation rate of MO by Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs-5 decreased to 69.4 %. In comparison to experiments without free radical scavengers, this decrease of 30 % in degradation rate was most pronounced, indicating that h + played a predominant role in the degradation process. When N2, TBA and AgNO3 were introduced into the system, the degradation rates of the dyes decreased to 93.6 %, 91.9 % and 90.3 %. This suggests that the ·O2 −, ·OH and e − also participated in the degradation reaction, with e − playing a more significant role. These results highlight the pivotal role of e − - h + synergy in dye degradation.

To further corroborate the generation of free radicals during the degradation process, ESR spectroscopy tests were conducted, as exhibited in Fig. (11b-d). In a well-illuminated environment for h +, ·O2 − and ·OH were detected, confirming the production of these three active species under visible light irradiation, actively participating in the degradation reaction. Electrons tend to accumulate in the conduction band of g-C₃N₄, where they react with O2 to form ·O2 -, resulting in the strong signal peak observed for ·O2 -. A significant concentration of holes is primarily found in the valence band of Ag₃PO₄. These holes directly oxidize and degrade pollutants while also indirectly oxidizing H2O to form ∙OH, contributing to the degradation reaction. As a result, the signal peak area for ·OH was relatively smaller. Under dark conditions, h + exhibited a stronger signal than in the presence of light, and this signal weakened as the light source was turned on. This weakening may be attributed to more h + directly participating in the degradation reaction, and the signal peak gradually diminishing over time, the signals for ·O2 − and ·OH were nearly undetectable. This further underscores that h + primarily functions as the dominant active species in the degradation process, with ·O2 − and ·OH being generated under visible light irradiation, acting as auxiliary active species to facilitate the reaction.

After conducting a series of characterization analysis, the following degradation mechanism can be inferred. Calculated via UV–Vis DRS, the band gaps of Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄ were determined to be 2.41 eV and 2.95 eV, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 12 , the charge transfer mechanism among Ag₃PO₄, g-C₃N₄ and CQDs is akin to that of a Z-scheme heterojunction [80]. Ag₃PO₄ possesses a lower the conduction band position (CB = 0.65 eV) compared to g-C₃N₄ (CB = −0.06 eV), while its valence band position (VB = 3.06 eV) is higher than that of g-C₃N₄ (VB = 2.89 eV). Electrons are transferred from the conduction band of Ag₃PO₄ to the valence band of g-C₃N₄ via the electron bridge provided by CQDs. Subsequently, they recombine with the holes in the valence band and ultimately migrate to the conduction band of g-C₃N₄ [81]. Holes play a pivotal role in the degradation reaction, with the valence band potential of Ag₃PO₄ and g-C₃N₄ being higher than the reduction potential (H2O/·OH = 2.38 V) [48]. Thus, holes formed in the valence band can directly oxidize and degrade pollutants. Additionally, H2O can be oxidized to form ·OH, which can further participate in the degradation of pollutants under visible light [82]. The conduction band potential of g-C₃N₄ is lower than the REDOX potential of (O2/·O2 − = −0.046 V) [48], and the photoexcited electrons eventually accumulate in the conduction band of g-C₃N₄, reducing O2 to form ·O2 −. These ·O2 − species further participate in the degradation reaction. The reaction Eqs. (7–9) involved in the process are as follows:

CQDs act as electron bridges, providing an electron transport pathway and enhancing the separation efficiency of e − and h +. Under light excitation, the catalyst continuously generates e − and h +, allowing for the continuous degradation of pollutants.

In summary, this study presents a straightforward synthesis of the Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs through physical mixing. Under visible light irradiation, this composite exhibits outstanding degradation performance for MO, MB and RhB, with a degradation rate reaching 99 % after 120 min of illumination. The highest degradation rate constant 0.02743 min−1 was achieved when mAg₃PO₄:mg-C₃N ₄ = 1:1, and 5 mg/L CQDs solution was loaded. The study investigated the impact of pollutant concentration, catalyst dosage, and solution pH on the degradation and confirmed the catalyst's stability by conducting tests over five cycles. Characterization analysis revealed that the composite catalyst Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs constructs a Z-scheme heterostructure with CQDs serving as an electron bridge. Several factors contributed to its enhanced catalytic activity: CQDs, as a cocatalyst, increases the specific surface area of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄, thus augmenting active sites on the catalyst's surface. The catalyst's band gap structure is optimized, reducing e − - h + recombination. CQDs provide an electron transport pathway, enhancing the charge transfer rate. Free radical capture experiments and ESR tests confirmed that h + is the primary active substance in the degradation process of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs, working synergistically with e − to boost the catalytic activity of Ag₃PO₄/g-C₃N₄/CQDs. In summary, these findings demonstrate the promising potential of coal-based CQDs as a valuable nanomaterial in the field of visible light catalytic degradation.

Yijun Wang and Mei Li conceived and designed experiments; Yijun Wang conducted experiments; Mei Li contributed reagents/materials and analytical tools, Meijuan Ding and Ziyu Li assisted in data analysis and processing, Yijun Wang and Mei Li jointly wrote the first draft.
